unique_basics.name: unique_ptr: Basics
unique_basics.explanation: std::unique_ptr<T> is a move-only smart pointer that owns its object. make_unique<T>(args) allocates on the heap and returns a unique_ptr. .get() returns the raw pointer; *ptr dereferences it; .reset() releases. When the unique_ptr goes out of scope, the heap object is automatically freed.

unique_move.name: unique_ptr: Move
unique_move.explanation: unique_ptr cannot be copied — ownership is transferred with std::move(). After the move, the source unique_ptr is null. This enforces exclusive ownership: exactly one unique_ptr owns the object.

unique_copy_err.name: unique_ptr: Copy Error
unique_copy_err.explanation: unique_ptr's copy constructor is deleted — it cannot be copied. auto q = p; is a compile error: 'call to deleted function'. Use std::move(p) to transfer ownership instead. This guarantees that only one owner exists at any point.

shared_basics.name: shared_ptr: Basics
shared_basics.explanation: std::shared_ptr<T> is reference-counted: copying it increments the count; destroying a copy decrements it. The heap object is freed only when the count reaches 0. This demo traces the full lifecycle: 1 owner -> 2 owners -> 1 owner -> 0 owners (freed). A weak_ptr observer lets us read use_count() safely after the last shared_ptr is gone.

shared_copy.name: shared_ptr: Copy
shared_copy.explanation: Copying a shared_ptr increments the reference count. Both sp1 and sp2 own the same heap object. use_count() is 2 while both are alive; drops to 1 when sp2 goes out of scope.

weak_basics.name: weak_ptr: Basics
weak_basics.explanation: std::weak_ptr<T> observes a shared_ptr without contributing to its reference count. Creating a weak_ptr does not increase use_count(). To access the object, call .lock() which returns a shared_ptr (or empty if expired). use_count() of the shared_ptr remains 1 after creating the weak_ptr.

weak_expired.name: weak_ptr: Expired
weak_expired.explanation: When the last shared_ptr owning an object is destroyed, the weak_ptr that observed it becomes expired. wp.expired() returns true; wp.lock() returns an empty shared_ptr. See the next tab (Cycle Break) for why this matters: weak_ptr is the standard fix for shared_ptr reference cycles.

weak_cycle.name: weak_ptr: Cycle Break
weak_cycle.explanation: Problem: A holds shared_ptr<B> and B holds shared_ptr<A>. Neither use_count ever reaches 0, so neither destructor runs — memory leak. Fix: make one link a weak_ptr (it does not increment use_count). Proof: select 'Cycle (leak)' — no destructor output. Select 'Fix (weak_ptr)' — both destructors fire.

value_map.Cycle (leak): std::shared_ptr<A> a_ptr;  // keeps A alive — use_count never 0
value_map.Fix (weak_ptr): std::weak_ptr<A>   a_ptr;  // does not keep A alive — cycle broken
